title: Challenges in developing broad-spectrum viral attachment inhibitors targeting heparan sulfates and sialic acids.
target: Cell surface glycans (heparan sulfates and sialic acids) as viral attachment factors
method: Review and synthesis of decades of approaches including enzymatic/genetic glycan removal, glycan masking, engineered decoy receptors, and multivalent inhibitors tested against broad-spectrum viral attachment
hypothesis: Blocking conserved glycan-mediated viral attachment interactions would yield broad-spectrum antivirals effective enough for clinical use
outcomeSummary: Despite promising in vitro results across multiple independent strategies over decades, no glycan-targeted attachment inhibitor has advanced to routine clinical use.
whyItFailed: Insufficient potency in vivo, off-target effects from targeting ubiquitous host glycans, risk of viral resistance, and practical challenges with administration routes
reuseAngle: Future antiviral attachment-inhibitor programs targeting host glycans should design early for potency, selectivity, and delivery constraints identified here, rather than repeating in vitro-only validation that historically failed to translate clinically.

title: Repurposing ALK inhibitors as influenza and corona virus antivirals targeting lymphocyte tyrosine kinase (LTK).
authors: Tjärnhage E, Våtsveen TK, Raki M, Nemazee D, Farhan H, Munthe L, Grodeland G.
target: LTK (lymphocyte tyrosine kinase), ER-to-Golgi trafficking host pathway exploited by influenza and SARS-CoV-2
method: Screened multiple repurposed ALK inhibitors (ceritinib, crizotinib, entrectinib, ensartinib, brigatinib, alectinib, lorlatinib) for in vitro antiviral activity against influenza and SARS-CoV-2, then tested a subset in vivo using lethal viral challenge mouse models
hypothesis: Since these ALK inhibitors also inhibit LTK, and LTK is required for ER-to-Golgi trafficking exploited by viruses, all tested LTK-inhibiting ALK inhibitors were expected to reduce viral load in vitro and protect against lethal viral challenge in vivo
outcomeSummary: Lorlatinib failed to inhibit influenza or SARS-CoV-2 in vitro despite being an ALK/LTK inhibitor, unlike the other six compounds tested; additionally, only some of the in vitro-active compounds (crizotinib, brigatinib, ceritinib) showed protective effects in vivo, implying others that worked in vitro did not translate to in vivo protection or were not effective/tested successfully in vivo.
whyItFailed: Not fully explained in the abstract; likely due to differences in LTK-inhibitory potency/selectivity, pharmacokinetics, or off-target activity among the ALK inhibitors, with lorlatinib potentially lacking sufficient LTK inhibition or relevant off-target antiviral activity compared to the other compounds
reuseAngle: This highlights that structurally similar host-targeted kinase inhibitors can have divergent antiviral efficacy despite shared nominal target inhibition, so future host-targeted antiviral screens should systematically test multiple compounds within a drug class and confirm in vitro hits with in vivo validation rather than assuming class-wide efficacy from a shared molecular target.

title: CC-90009, a Cereblon E3 Ligase Modulator, Exhibits Antiviral Efficacy Against JEV In Vitro and In Vivo via Targeted Degradation of GSPT1 and Viral NS5 Protein.
authors: He Z, Chen Y, Xia B, Cheng Z, Zhao P, Qi Z, Zhu Y.
target: Targeted protein degraders (TPDs)/CRBN E3 ligase modulators screened for anti-JEV activity
method: Screening of five distinct targeted protein degrader compounds for antiviral activity against JEV in human neuroblastoma SH-SY5Y cells
hypothesis: Multiple CRBN-based protein degraders would show antiviral efficacy against JEV, given the class's known ability to induce degradation of host factors relevant to viral replication
outcomeSummary: Only one of the five TPDs tested (CC-90009) significantly inhibited JEV infection; the other four degraders showed no meaningful antiviral effect in this cell model
whyItFailed: The inactive TPDs likely lacked the specific molecular glue activity needed to engage GSPT1 (or an equivalent host factor) in a way that disrupts the viral NS5 complex, suggesting target/mechanism specificity rather than general degrader potency determines antiviral effect
reuseAngle: Future antiviral TPD screens should note that broad CRBN-modulator activity does not guarantee antiviral efficacy, so early-stage screens should prioritize compounds validated to engage specific host-viral protein complexes like GSPT1-NS5 rather than assuming class-wide effects.
